one_line_takeaway: Reasoning does confer partial robustness to multi-turn social/rhetorical pressure (8/9 models beat a non-reasoning baseline), but reasoning models are systematically overconfident (r=0.07 confidence-correctness correlation), which causes confidence-gated defenses like CARG to fail and even underperform random confidence embedding.
key_finding: 8 of 9 reasoning models show significantly higher PWC than the GPT-4o baseline (Welch's t, p<0.05, d=0.12-0.40; aggregate mean delta=+0.064, t=5.02, p=0.001), with Acc_init 82-95% vs. GPT-4o's 78.14% and Acc_avg 95-99% vs. 91.29%. Claude-4.5 is the sole exception: highest Acc_init (94.86%) but no significant PWC improvement (t=-0.97, p=0.33) and significantly lower Acc_avg (t=-4.62, p<0.001, d=-0.27), driven by disproportionate Oscillating trajectories (94 instances, ~3x the next-highest model) and total flips (347, an order of magnitude above GPT-5.1's 33). Across all models, Delayed Recovery is the dominant flip pattern. Misleading suggestions (A3, an explicit wrong alternative) are universally among the most effective attacks; social-pressure attacks (consensus, expert appeal) show model-specific effectiveness (e.g., consensus appeal is uniquely potent against Claude-4.5); expert appeal (A6) is the weakest attack overall. Self-Doubt and Social Conformity together account for 338+337=675 of 1347 total failures (50%); Suggestion Hijacking is rarest overall (155) but dominates Grok-4.1's failures (44 of 101, 44%). Confidence calibration in reasoning models is poor: point-biserial correlation between confidence and correctness r=0.07 (p=0.079, n.s.), ROC-AUC=0.57 (barely above chance), confidence scores cluster tightly (mean 93.5%, SD 4.4%, range 75-100%). Flip rate is highest for low-confidence-tercile correct answers (9.3%) versus medium (6.7%) and high (6.2%) terciles -- meaning CARG-style confidence gating protects the responses that need it least. Consequently, for GPT-5.1, CARG with answer_only confidence (Acc_avg 98.29%, PWC 1.769) and overall confidence (98.39%, 1.773) underperform the no-CARG baseline (98.50%, 1.765), while random confidence embedding outperforms both (Acc_avg 98.88%, PWC 1.778).
core_contribution: An empirical, hypothesis-driven study (not a new method or attack) of whether large reasoning models (LRMs) maintain correct answers under an 8-round multi-turn adversarial follow-up protocol. The authors evaluate nine frontier reasoning models (GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2, GPT-OSS-120B, Grok-4.1, Grok-3, DeepSeek-R1, Claude-4.5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Qwen-3) against a GPT-4o instruction-tuned baseline, using the MT-Consistency dataset and attack taxonomy from prior work (Li et al. 2025b). Three original analyses are contributed on top of that existing protocol: (1) a robustness comparison via Initial Accuracy / Follow-up Accuracy / Position-Weighted Consistency (PWC) with Welch's t-tests; (2) a 7-pattern trajectory taxonomy (No Flip, Immediate/Delayed Recovery, Delayed Sustained, Oscillating, Terminal Capitulation, Double Flip) plus a 5-mode failure taxonomy (Self-Doubt, Social Conformity, Suggestion Hijacking, Emotional Susceptibility, Reasoning Fatigue) derived from qualitative trace inspection; and (3) a test of whether Confidence-Aware Response Generation (CARG), a defense shown effective for standard LLMs in prior work, transfers to reasoning models -- finding that it does not, and diagnosing why via confidence-correctness correlation analysis and an ablation of three confidence-elicitation strategies (overall, answer_only, random).
threat_model: Not an exploit/jailbreak paper -- the 'attack' is social/rhetorical multi-turn pressure applied to a model that has already given a correct, well-reasoned answer to a factual multiple-choice question. An 8-round scripted adversarial protocol (randomized order per question) applies 8 follow-up types drawn from Table 1: closed-ended doubt ('Are you sure?'), open-ended disagreement, misleading suggestion of a concrete wrong alternative answer, emotional appeal, impolite tone, expert-authority appeal, consensus/social-proof appeal, and false agreement with a wrong answer. The adversary is a conversational user (or simulated user), not a system-level attacker; there is no prompt injection, jailbreak, or external-document threat vector. Success is measured as the model flipping from a correct to an incorrect answer.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation is restricted to factual multiple-choice questions with objectively verifiable answers (MT-Consistency), which may not generalize to open-ended generation, tool-augmented/RAG systems, or domains with ambiguous correctness, and prompts are primarily English; (2) the 8-round attack protocol covers 8 common social/rhetorical follow-up types but does not exhaust real-world adversarial interactions such as adaptive attackers, prompt injection via external documents, or domain-specific misinformation, so the vulnerability profiles are indicative rather than comprehensive; (3) CARG evaluation covers only log-probability-based confidence extraction (overall/answer_only) plus a random control, omitting other uncertainty signals (self-consistency, verifier-based confidence, calibrated abstention), and the failure-mode taxonomy is derived from qualitative inspection of flipped trajectories, introducing potential subjectivity despite operational definitions.

datasets_used: MT-Consistency (700 four-option MCQ questions, 39 subjects grouped into 7 domain clusters, from Li et al. 2025b)
MMLU (57 subjects, source of MT-Consistency questions)
TruthfulQA (38 categories, source of MT-Consistency questions)
quantitative_results: Acc_init range for reasoning models: 82.29%-94.86% vs GPT-4o baseline 78.14%
Acc_avg range for reasoning models: 86.31%-98.92% vs GPT-4o baseline 91.29%
PWC range for reasoning models: 1.675-1.797 vs GPT-4o baseline 1.693
8/9 models significantly higher PWC than baseline, Cohen's d = 0.12 (DeepSeek-R1) to 0.40 (Grok-4.1)
Aggregate one-sample t-test: mean PWC delta = +0.064, t=5.02, p=0.001
Claude-4.5: PWC t=-0.97 p=0.33 (not significant); Acc_avg t=-4.62 p<0.001 d=-0.27
Claude-4.5 total flips = 347, Oscillating = 94; DeepSeek-R1 total flips = 260; GPT-OSS total flips = 30; GPT-5.1 total flips = 33
Self-Doubt = 338 failures, Social Conformity = 337 failures (50% of 1347 total failures combined)
Suggestion Hijacking = 155 total, but 44/101 (44%) of Grok-4.1's failures
Flip rate by confidence tercile: Low (<33rd pct) 9.3% (n=193), Medium 6.7% (n=193), High (>67th pct) 6.2% (n=193)
GPT-5.1 CARG comparison: Baseline (no CARG) Acc_avg 98.50%/PWC 1.765; overall CARG 98.39%/1.773; answer_only CARG 98.29%/1.769; random CARG 98.88%/1.778
baselines_compared: GPT-4o (instruction-tuned, non-reasoning baseline, reported as best-performing model in Li et al. 2025b)
No-CARG (no-intervention) baseline for the CARG ablation
